title: Le passé composé — introduction
description: The basic French past tense: avoir + past participle, for regular -er verbs.

cards.notes: Regular **-er** verb. Past participle: **parlé** (just swap -er for -é). Sounds the same as the infinitive — context disambiguates.
Past participle: **regardé**. *J'ai regardé un film* — I watched a movie.
Past participle: **écouté**. Starts with a vowel — elision: *j'ai écouté*.
Past participle: **habité**. The **h** is silent — elision applies: *j'ai habité*.
Past participle: **acheté**. *J'ai acheté du pain* — I bought some bread.
Past participle: **travaillé**. *Hier j'ai travaillé* — yesterday I worked.
Past participle: **aimé**. *J'ai aimé ce film* — I liked this movie.
Common time marker that pairs with the passé composé: *Hier j'ai mangé une pizza* — yesterday I ate a pizza.
Another past-time marker. Also: *ce soir* (this evening), *la semaine dernière* (last week).

steps.title: Talking about the past
steps.body:
# Le passé composé

French has several past tenses. The most common — the one you'll use in everyday speech — is the **passé composé**. It covers both *I spoke* AND *I have spoken* in English; French doesn't make that distinction.

The formula has two pieces:

> **avoir** (conjugated for the subject) + **past participle**

- *J'**ai parlé** français.* — I spoke French.
- *Tu **as mangé** ?* — Have you eaten? / Did you eat?
- *Elle **a regardé** un film.* — She watched a movie.
- *Nous **avons travaillé** hier.* — We worked yesterday.

Lesson 04 covered the present tense of *avoir*. You already know it — half the work is done.

This lesson sticks to **regular -er verbs**. They're the largest group in French (≈90% of all verbs) and they form their past participle the same easy way every time.

steps.title: Making the past participle
steps.body:
## Regular -er verbs: drop the -er, add -é

| Infinitive | Past participle |
|---|---|
| parl**er** (to speak) | parl**é** |
| mang**er** (to eat) | mang**é** |
| regard**er** (to watch) | regard**é** |
| écout**er** (to listen) | écout**é** |
| habit**er** (to live) | habit**é** |
| achet**er** (to buy) | achet**é** |

That's it. Every regular -er verb works this way.

Pronunciation trap: **parler** and **parlé** sound identical (both "par-LAY"). You hear which one is meant from context — is there an *ai*/*as*/*a* in front? Then it's the participle.

- *Je parle français.* — I speak French. (present, no avoir)
- *J'ai parlé français.* — I spoke French. (passé composé)

exercise.prompt: Match each infinitive with its past participle.
What is the past participle of **travailler** (to work)?
exercise.hint: Drop -er, add -é. The result has the same accent pattern as *parlé*, *mangé*.

steps.title: Putting it together
steps.body:
## The full conjugation

Using **parler** (parlé) as the example:

| Subject | avoir | + parlé |
|---|---|---|
| je | **ai** | j'ai parlé |
| tu | **as** | tu as parlé |
| il / elle | **a** | il a parlé |
| nous | **avons** | nous avons parlé |
| vous | **avez** | vous avez parlé |
| ils / elles | **ont** | ils ont parlé |

Swap *parlé* for any other -er participle: *mangé*, *regardé*, *écouté*, *acheté*, *habité*, *travaillé*, *aimé*, ...

**Word order** in a statement: subject + avoir + past participle + rest. The past participle stays right after *avoir* — nothing slips in between.

- *J'**ai bien** mangé.* — I ate well. (*bien* before the participle)
- *Elle **n'a pas** travaillé.* — She didn't work. (negation around *a*)

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I ate yesterday.* (Use the passé composé. Word order: subject + avoir + participle + time.)
Arrange to mean: *She watched a film yesterday.*
exercise.hint: Subject + form of *avoir* + participle + object + time marker.

steps.title: Elision with avoir
steps.body:
## Don't forget the elisions

Three elisions to watch for in the passé composé:

1. **Je → J'** before *ai*: always *j'ai*, never *je ai*.
2. **Past participles starting with a vowel** trigger liaison from *a* / *ont* / etc. — pronunciation only, spelling unchanged. *Il a écouté* → spoken as "il a-z-écouté" (rough).
3. **Negation** wraps *avoir*, not the whole verb: *Je **n'ai pas** parlé*, not *Je n'ai pas parlé*. The *n'* elides before *ai*.

For A1: just say *J'ai parlé*, *Je n'ai pas parlé* (positive vs negative). The other patterns come later but it's worth recognising them in the wild.

exercise.prompt: How do you say *I did not eat*?
exercise.hint: Negation wraps *avoir* (the conjugated verb): *ne ... pas* around *ai*.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *We listened to music this morning.*
accept: Nous avons écouté de la musique ce matin.
Nous avons écouté de la musique ce matin
nous avons écouté de la musique ce matin
distractors: Nous écoutons de la musique ce matin.
Nous avons écouter de la musique ce matin.
Nous sommes écouté de la musique ce matin.

exercise.prompt: Match each French time expression with its English meaning.
Arrange to mean: *I bought a book yesterday.*
Translate: *They liked the film.* (Use masculine *they*. Past tense, passé composé.)
